The surgeon maintains traction while performing lateral dissection, aiming to enter the previously dissected medial plane.
The surgeon identifies the ureter during the dissection, noting the absence of stents in this case.
I'm pretty selective in my utilization of stents, and on this patient's preoperative imaging, I didn't think it was necessary.
The surgeon enters the medially dissected plane, revealing the psoas tendon and the ureter in the surgical field.
The surgeon begins lateral mobilization towards the splenic flexure, although full mobilization is not performed in this case.
The surgeon dissects tissue from the left pelvic sidewall down to the upper rectum, enhancing mobility for the upcoming anastomosis.
The surgeon selects the distal transection margin and proceeds to identify the proximal margin for the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon clears the distal upper rectal mesentery, removing connective tissue to expose the underlying structures.
The surgeon identifies the area for the proximal margin, located just above the transection of the inferior mesenteric artery, and prepares to extend the dissection towards this margin.
The surgeon assesses perfusion using indocyanine green (ICG) after taking the mesentery to both planned margins, ensuring adequate blood flow to the tissue.
The surgeon proceeds to transect the bowel distally and then proximally, completing the separation at the previously selected margins.

The surgeon begins with a three OB lock suture in the midline and proceeds to suture towards the near side using an over-and-over stitch technique on the posterior row. As the suturing progresses anteriorly, the surgeon switches to an inverting Limbert suture.
The surgeon transitions the suture technique, working with the original suture to bring it back towards themselves, ensuring the confluence of the two sutures is not too close to a corner.
The surgeon inverts the two lips of mucosa and places an additional suture above the previous one to ensure proper closure.
The surgeon ties the two sutures together, completing the anastomosis. Following this, a leak test is performed to ensure the integrity of the anastomosis.

The surgeon demonstrates the anastomosis, showing no tension on the sutured area. The pelvis is flooded, and a rigid scope is inserted below the surgeon's position to check for leaks, revealing no bubbles. The scope is then passed above and through the widely patent anastomosis.
